articles.title: Ch 1 — Holding a family meeting & caregiving agreement
content: Getting the family together to agree, out loud, on who looks after the elder and how — so care does not fall on one exhausted person or fall through the cracks entirely.
When caregiving is patchy or unclear (Q6 = 2), or when one family member is carrying everything and others are absent.

ul: Find out first, quietly, who actually does the caring now — not who is supposed to, but who really does. It is often one woman: a wife, a daughter-in-law.
Ask the elder who they trust and who they want help from. Their answer comes first.
Pick a time the working family members are home — usually evening. Tell them it is a short, friendly conversation about amma/appa, not a complaint session.
Sit with the whole family together. Name the care tasks plainly: meals, medicines, bathing, toilet, company, money. Ask: who can take which?
Push gently for backup. If only one person is named for everything, that person will break. Ask who is the second pair of hands.
Write the agreement down simply — who does what, on what days — and read it back so everyone hears it. Leave a copy in the house.
Set a date to check back. An agreement nobody revisits dies in a week.

content: In a 180-sq-ft home with everyone working daily wage, there may be no slack at all — the family is not unwilling, they are stretched to breaking. Do not moralise. Look for what is actually possible, and fill the rest with kitchen, centre, and your own visits.
Talk about the elder, with the elder present and included — never over their head as if they are furniture. Ask their view at every step. They ran a household once; they are not a task on a list.
Do not shame the family or take sides in front of everyone. If you turn the meeting into blame, the door closes and the elder loses their advocate inside the house.
The family has named who does what, there is a written copy in the home, a backup person exists, and you have a date to check it is holding.

articles.title: Ch 2 — Training a caregiver
content: Teaching the family member (or paid helper) who looks after the elder the practical skills to do it safely and without hurting themselves or the elder.
Whenever an elder needs hands-on help — bathing, lifting, medicines, a bedridden elder. Especially when a fall, illness, or new dependency has just changed things.

ul: Watch how the caregiver does it now, before teaching. They have been managing; respect that, and build on it.

Teach the few things that matter most, by showing, not lecturing: how to help an elder up without hurting either back; how to turn a bedridden elder; how to keep skin dry; how to give medicines on time.
Have the caregiver do it while you watch, and correct gently. People learn with their hands, not their ears.
Write or draw the medicine routine and stick it on the wall where it is seen daily.
Tell them plainly what danger signs mean 'call me or the PHC today' — fever, a wound, not passing urine, sudden confusion, a fall.
Come back within a week to check the skills stuck, and to encourage. Praise what they are doing right.

content: The caregiver is often herself tired, unwell, or also caring for children. Teaching her is also caring for her — ask how she is managing. A caregiver who feels seen will keep going; one who feels used will not.
Teach in a way that lets the elder keep doing what they still can. Never train the caregiver to do FOR the elder what the elder can still do themselves — that steals independence and speeds decline.
The caregiver can do the key tasks safely while you watch, knows the danger signs and whom to call, and the medicine routine is on the wall.

articles.title: Ch 3 — Arranging caregiver respite & support
content: Giving the main caregiver a break and a circle of support, so they do not burn out and start — without meaning to — neglecting the elder.
When the caregiver shows strain (Q6b = 2) or burnout (Q6b = 3): exhausted, short-tempered, sleepless, unwell, or saying they cannot manage.

ul: Talk to the caregiver alone, kindly. Ask how THEY are — not how the elder is. Let them say the hard thing.
Find a few hours of relief: another family member to take a shift, a neighbour to sit, or the elder attending the centre for a day.
Connect them to other caregivers — the caregiver support group at the centre. Knowing others carry the same weight lightens it.
If burnout is severe, treat it as urgent: arrange the respite within two weeks, bring in the supervisor, and look at sharing the load across the family.
Check the medicines and the elder's basic care are still happening while the caregiver is stretched — burnout is where quiet neglect begins.
Keep checking on the caregiver, not just the elder, at every visit.

content: The daughter-in-law caring for a bedridden elder while raising children is at the highest risk and the least likely to complain — complaining can cause family trouble for her. Ask her directly and privately. An aged spouse caring for another aged spouse is just as fragile.
Respite is not a luxury or a weakness. Say so plainly to a guilty caregiver: 'Resting so you can keep caring is part of caring.'
The caregiver has real, regular relief; is connected to other caregivers; and the elder's basic care is confirmed to be holding.

articles.title: Ch 4 — When there is no caregiver
content: Building a patchwork of support around an elder who lives alone or has no one reliable — so they are not left to manage a failing body by themselves.
When the elder lives alone with no dependable support (Q21 = 3), or the critical flag 'no dependable caregiver' is raised.

ul: Map who is already nearby: a neighbour who looks in, a shopkeeper who knows them, a distant relative, the settlement leader. Even loose connections matter.
Ask the elder who they would call in trouble — and whether that person actually answers.
Build a daily touch-point: the community kitchen meal delivery doubles as a daily check that the elder is alright; centre attendance if they can walk there.
Identify one willing neighbour as the 'first call' and make sure they have your number and the elder agrees.
Where there is genuinely no one and the elder is dependent, raise with the supervisor whether a paid community caregiver (a trusted local woman, small honorarium) can be arranged.
Make your own visits more frequent and reliable — for this elder, you are part of the safety net.

content: An elder living alone is not always lonely or in danger — some are at peace and capable. Do not impose help that wounds their pride. But a dependent elder living alone with no one is a quiet emergency. Tell the two apart by watching, and by asking them.
Living alone can be a choice and a strength, not only a problem. Honour the elder who manages alone and wants to. Offer the net without insisting they are helpless.
The elder has at least one reliable contact within a few minutes' reach, a daily touch-point exists, and you and a named neighbour both watch over them.
